Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

IMPLICIT BIAS OF PROJECTED SUBGRADIENT METHOD GIVES PROVABLE ROBUST RECOVERY OF SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION
**Paris Giampouras, Benjamin D. Haeffele and René Vidal** Mathematical Institute for Data Science Johns Hopkins University Baltimore, MD, USA {parisg,bhaeffele,rvidal}@jhu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) is the problem of learning a subspace from sample data points corrupted by outliers. Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) is a robust subspace recovery method that aims to find a basis for the orthogonal complement of the subspace by minimizing the sum of the distances of the points to the subspaces subject to orthogonality constraints on the basis. Prior work has shown that DPCP can provably recover the correct subspace in the presence of outliers as long as the true dimension of the subspace is known. In this paper, we show that if the orthogonality constraints –adopted in previous DPCP formulations– are relaxed and random initialization is used instead of spectral one, DPCP can provably recover a subspace of _unknown dimension_ . Specifically, we propose a very simple algorithm based on running multiple instances of a projected sub-gradient descent method (PSGM), with each problem instance seeking to find one vector in the null space of the subspace. We theoretically prove that under mild conditions this approach succeeds with high probability. In particular, we show that 1) all of the problem instances will converge to a vector in the nullspace of the subspace and 2) the ensemble of problem instance solutions will be sufficiently diverse to fully span the nullspace of the subspace thus also revealing its true unknown codimension. We provide empirical results that corroborate our theoretical results and showcase the remarkable implicit rank regularization behavior of the PSGM algorithm that allows us to perform RSR without knowing the subspace dimension.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) refers to the problem of identifying an underlying linear subspace (with dimension less than the ambient data dimension) from sample data points that are potentially corrupted with outliers (i.e., points that do not lie in the linear subspace). Many methods for RSR have been proposed in the literature over the past several years (Xu et al., 2012; You et al., 2017a; Lerman & Maunu, 2018). Formulations based on convex relaxations and decompositions of the data matrix into a low-rank matrix plus a matrix of sparse corruptions – either entrywise-sparse corruptions as in Candès et al. (2011) or columnwise-sparse corruptions as in Xu et al. (2012); McCoy & Tropp (2011) – can, in certain situations, be shown to provably recover the true subspace when the dimension is unknown. However, these theoretical guarantees often require the dimension of the subspace, _d_ , to be significantly less than the ambient dimension of the data, _D_ , and these methods are not suitable for the more challenging regime of subspaces of high relative dimension (i.e., when _D[d][≈]_[1][).]

**Dual Principal Component Pursuit.** Recently, progress has been made towards solving the RSR problem in the high relative dimension regime by a formulation termed Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP). As implied by its name, DPCP follows a _dual_ perspective of RSR by aiming to recover a basis for the orthogonal complement of the inliers’ subspace. As shown in (Tsakiris & Vidal, 2018), DPCP is provably robust in recovering subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key limitation of DPCP is that it requires _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension.

DPCP was first formulated by Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) for handling subspaces of codimension _c_ = _D − d_ equal to 1 (i.e., the subspace is a hyperplane with dimension _d_ = _D −_ 1). In this case, DPCP is formulated as the optimization problem

which is nonconvex due to the spherical constraint imposed on the normal vector _**b** ∈_ S _[D][−]_[1] of the _D −_ 1 dimensional hyperplane. Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) showed that the global minimizer of (1) is a normal vector of the underlying true hyperplane when both inliers and outliers are well-distributed _or_ the ratio between the number of inliers and number of outliers is sufficiently small. Following a probabilistic point of view, (Zhu et al., 2018) presented an improved theoretical analysis of DPCP giving further insights on the remarkable robustness of DPCP in recovering the true underlying subspaces even in datasets heavily corrupted by outliers. Moreover, the authors introduced a projected subgradient method which converges to a normal vector of the true subspace at a linear rate.

**Recursive DPCP for known** _c >_ 1. Zhu et al. (2018) also proposed an extension to DPCP to subspaces with codimension _c >_ 1 via a projected subgradient algorithm that tries to learn _c_ normal vectors to the subspace in a recursive manner. Specifically, after convergence to a normal vector, the projected subgradient algorithm is initialized with a vector _orthogonal_ to the previously estimated normal vector. However, for that approach to be successful, knowledge of the true subspace codimension _c_ becomes critical. Specifically, if an underestimate of the true codimension _c_ is assumed the recovered basis for the null space, _**B**_[ˆ] , will fail to span the whole null space, _S⊥_ . On the other hand, an overestimate of _c_ will lead to columns of _**B**_[ˆ] corresponding to vectors that lie in _S_ .

**Orthogonal DPCP for known** _c_ . Zhu et al. (2019) proposed an alternative to (1) which attempts to solve for _c_ normal vectors to the subspace at once by minimizing the sum of the distances from the points to the subspace:

The authors also proposed an optimization algorithm based on the projected Riemannian subgradient method (RSGM), which builds on similar ideas as the projected subgradient method of Zhu et al. (2018) and enjoys a linear converge rate when the step size is selected based on a geometrically diminishing rule. Ding et al. (2021) provided a geometric analysis of (2) which shows the merits of DPCP in handling a) datasets highly contaminated by outliers (in the order of _M_ = _O_ ( _N_[2] )) and b) subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key shortcoming of this approach is that, because all minimizers of (2) are orthogonal matrices, a prerequisite for recovering the correct orthogonal complement of the inliers subspace is the _a priori_ knowledge of the true codimension _c_ (see Fig. 1).

**Contributions.** In this work, we address this key limitation by proposing a framework that allows us to perform robust subspace recovery in the high relative subspace dimension regime **without** requiring _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension. In particular, our proposed approach is based on the simple idea of solving multiple, parallel instances of the DPCP formulation for subspaces of codimension one,

where _c[′]_ is assumed to be an upper bound of _c_ , i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ . Contrary to (2), the objective function in (3) decouples over the columns _**b** i_ of matrix _**B**_ = [ _**b**_ 1 _**b**_ 2 _· · ·_ _**b** c′_ ] and thus can be solved in a parallel manner by independently applying a projected subgradient algorithm (referred to as PSGM) from _c[′]_ different _random initializations_ . Moreover, we observe that with random initialization we can get vectors sufficiently spread on the sphere that lead PSGM (initialized with those vectors) to return normal vectors of _S_ . These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ . If _c[′] > c_ then PSGM will return _c[′] − c_ redundant vectors that will still lie in _S⊥_ yet they will be linearly dependent (see Figure 1). That being said, we show that this simple strategy permits us to robustly recover the true subspace even without knowledge of the true codimension _c_ .

As is detailed in Sections 3 and 4, this remarkable behavior of PSGM originates from the _implicit bias_ that is induced in the optimization problem due to a) the relaxation of _orthogonality constraints_ in (3) and b) the _random initialization_ scheme that is adopted. Our specific contributions are as follows:

Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a _scaled_ and _perturbed_ version of the initial matrix _**B**_[0] . ## 2 RELATED WORK

**Subspace Recovery.** Learning underlying low-dimensional subspace representations of data has been a central topic of interest in machine learning research. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) has been the most celebrated method of this kind and is based on the minimization of the perpendicular distances of the data points from the estimates linear subspace, Jolliffe & Cadima (2016). Albeit, it is originally formulated as nonconvex optimization problem, PCA can be easily solved in closed form using a singular value decomposition (SVD) operation (see e.g. Vidal et al. (2016)). Despite its great success, PCA is prone to failure when handling datasets that contain _outliers_ i.e., data points whose deviation from the inliers’ subspace is “large” in the _ℓ_ 2 norm sense.

**Robust Subspace Recovery (RSR).** To remedy this weakness of PCA _robust subspace recovery (RSR)_ methods attempt to identify the outliers in the dataset an recover the true underlying lowdimensional subspace of the inliers Lerman & Maunu (2018); Maunu et al. (2019). A classical approach to this problem is RANSAC Fischler & Bolles (1981), which is given a time budget and randomly chooses per iteration _d_ points and then fits a _d_ -dimensional subspace to those points and checks how the proposed subspace fits the remaining data points. RANSAC then outputs the subspace that agrees with the largest number of points. However, RANSAC’s reliance on randomly sampling points to propose subspaces can be highly inefficient when the number of outliers is high (as well as the fact that RANSAC also needs knowledge of the true subspace dimension, _d_ ). The need to tackle inherent shortcomings of RANSAC pertaining to computational complexity issues inspired alternative convex formulations of RSR, Xu et al. (2012); You et al. (2017b); Rahmani & Atia (2017); Zhang & Lerman (2014). In Xu et al. (2012) the authors decompose the data matrix as a sum of a low-rank and a column-sparse component. However, theoretical guarantees obtained for convex

formulations only hold for subspaces of relatively low-dimensional subspaces i.e., for _d ≪ D_ where _d_ and _D_ denote the subspace and the ambient dimension, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, existing RSR algorithms rely heavily on one of two key assumptions. 1) The subspace is very low-dimensional relative to the ambient dimension ( _d ≪ D_ ) or 2) _The subspace dimension is a priori known_ . Undoubtedly, the second hypothesis is rather strong in real-world applications, and many applications also do not satisfy the first assumption. Moreover, heuristic strategies for selecting the dimension of the subspace are hard to be applied in the RSR setting since they incur computationally prohibitive procedures, Lerman & Maunu (2018).

**Relation to Orthogonal Dictionary Learning (ODL).** Note that objective functions in the form of (3) show up beyond RSR problems i.e., in orthogonal dictionary learning (ODL), sparse blind deconvolution, etc., Qu et al. (2020). Specifically, based on a similar formulation the authors in Bai et al. (2019) proved that _c[′]_ = _O_ ( _c_ log _c_ ) independent random initial vectors suffice in order to recover with high probability a dictionary of size _D × c_ with high accuracy. In this paper we aim to recover a basis of the orthogonal complement of a subspace of unknown dimension instead of accurately estimating a dictionary hence our goal differs from that in Bai et al. (2019).

**Implicit bias in Robust Recovery Problems.** The notions of implicit bias and implicit regularization have been used interchangeably in the nonconvex optimization literature for describing the tendency of optimization algorithms to converge to global minima of minimal complexity with favorable generalization properties in overparameterized models, Gunasekar et al. (2018). In the context of robust recovery, the authors in You et al. (2020) showed that Robust PCA can be suitably reparametrized in such a way to favor low-rank and sparse solutions without using any explicit regularization. In this work, we use the term implicit bias for describing the convergence of DPCPPSGM to low-rank solutions, which are not necessarily global minimizers, that span the orthogonal complement of the subspace when a) orthogonality constraints in DPCP formulation are relaxed b) DPCP is overparameterized i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ and c) PSGM randomly initialized.

## 4 DUAL PRINCIPAL COMPONENT PURSUIT IN SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION

Current theoretical results provide guarantees for recovering the true inlier subspace, when the proposed algorithms know _a priori_ of the subspace codimension _c_ , which is a rather strong requirement and is far from being true in real word applications. Here we describe our proposed approach, which consists of removing the orthogonality constraint on _**B**_ , along with a theoretical analysis that gives guarantees of recovering the true underlying subspace even when the true codimension _c_ is unknown. First we analyze a continuous version of DPCP, which arises when the number of inliers and outliers are distributed according to continuous measures and their number tends to _∞_ . The continuous DPCP incurs an optimization problem with a benign landscape that allows us to better illustrate the favorable properties of DPCP-PSGM when it comes to the convergence of its iterates. Then we extend the results to the discrete case that deals with a finite number of inliers and outliers yielding a more challenging optimization landscape.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and well-motivated problem in robust subspace recovery (RSR): extending Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) to the setting where the subspace codimension is unknown, which is a genuine limitation of prior DPCP formulations. The proposed approach is appealingly simple—run multiple parallel instances of the codimension-one PSGM with random initializations—and the claim that implicit bias leads to automatic rank regularization is intellectually interesting and potentially impactful. However, several concerns arise from the introduction. First, the novelty relative to prior work (particularly Zhu et al. 2018, 2019, and Ding et al. 2021) hinges largely on the analysis of random initialization and the "diversity" of solutions, yet the introduction does not clearly articulate why the theoretical argument for spanning the nullspace is nontrivial or what technical innovations are required beyond existing DPCP analyses. Second, the writing is somewhat repetitive and dense with background, while key algorithmic and theoretical contributions are described only at a high level; specifically, the mechanism by which random initialization ensures the ensemble spans the full nullspace (rather than collapsing to a few dominant normal directions) deserves sharper exposition upfront. Third, the practical question of how one chooses the upper bound c′ and the computational trade-off versus simply using orthogonal DPCP with a conservative codimension estimate is not addressed in the introduction, leaving the practical significance somewhat unclear. Finally, the claim of "provable robust recovery" should be accompanied by a clearer preview of the assumptions (outlier-inlier ratio, distributional conditions, relation to D/d) to let the reader calibrate the strength of the guarantees against prior results. Tightening the contribution statement and sharpening the comparison with recursive and orthogonal DPCP would substantially strengthen the paper.

comments.title: Incomplete/unclear projection equation (11)
comments.explanation: Equation (11) claims P_{S^⊥}(b^{k+1}_i) = (1 - μ^k p c_D) P_{S^⊥}(b̂^k_i). For this to hold, the subgradient contribution from the outlier term, when projected onto S^⊥, must equal μ^k p c_D P_{S^⊥}(b̂^k_i). These steps are nontrivial and the equation is presented without derivation. More importantly, for the scaling factor (1 - μ^k p c_D) to be positive (necessary for the direction-preservation argument that follows), one needs μ^k < 1/(p c_D), which is not stated as a condition. If μ^k p c_D > 1, the sign of the projection flips, and the 'scaled version' interpretation fails. This boundary condition is not addressed.

comments.title: Inconsistent sphere dimension in c_{X,min} definition
comments.explanation: The definition of c_{X,min} uses minimization over b ∈ S^{d-1} ∩ S, while c_{X,max} uses b ∈ S^{D-1} ∩ S. Since S is a d-dimensional subspace of R^D, the intersection S^{D-1} ∩ S equals S^{d-1} ∩ S up to identification, so this is a notational inconsistency rather than a mathematical error, but the inconsistent exponents across two closely related definitions could confuse a reader trying to match these quantities to the referenced literature (Zhu et al. 2018), which uses S^{D-1} ∩ S throughout.

comments.title: Definition of $\eta_{\boldsymbol{X}}$ normalizes by $M$ instead of $N$
comments.explanation: The quantity $\eta_{\boldsymbol{X}}$ involves the inlier matrix $\boldsymbol{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times N}$, so the natural normalization would be $1/N$, not $1/M$. Indeed, the prose right after says '$\eta_{\boldsymbol{X}}\to 0$ as $N\to\infty$ provided that inliers are uniformly distributed', which only makes sense if the normalization involves $N$. This appears to be a typo in the definition.

comments.title: Condition in Theorem 7 uses quantity $c_d$ where one might expect $c_D$

comments.explanation: The summary established that as $M\to\infty$ with outliers uniform on $\mathbb{S}^{D-1}$, $c_{\boldsymbol{O},\max}\to c_D$ (not $c_d$). Writing '$c_{\boldsymbol{O},\max}\to c_d$' appears to be a subscript typo—the outlier permeance converges to the ambient-dimension constant $c_D$, not the subspace-dimension constant $c_d$. comments.title: Theorem 7 / Lemma 6 argument skips step from full-rank $\boldsymbol{A}$ to rank$(\boldsymbol{B}^*)=c$
comments.explanation: This is a standard Weyl-type bound giving rank$(\boldsymbol{A})=c'$. However, the theorem then claims rank$(\boldsymbol{B}^*)=c$, where $\boldsymbol{B}^* = P_{\mathbb{S}^{D-1}}(P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\boldsymbol{B}^0-\hat{\boldsymbol{\Delta}}))$. Full rank of $\boldsymbol{A}\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times c'}$ only guarantees rank$(P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\boldsymbol{A}))\geq c' - d$ in general. Since the columns of $\boldsymbol{B}^*$ lie in $\mathcal{S}^\perp$ (dimension $c$), the image has rank at most $c$. The argument implicitly requires $P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\boldsymbol{A})$ to have rank exactly $c$, which holds almost surely under genericity but is not spelled out.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about recursive DPCP with overestimated codimension
comments.quote: On the other hand, an overestimate of _c_ will lead to columns of _**B**_[ˆ] corresponding to vectors that lie in _S_ .
comments.explanation: The authors describe the recursive DPCP method: after finding one normal vector, the next iteration is initialized orthogonal to the previous estimates. If c' > c (overestimate), the claim is that extra columns will lie in S (the inlier subspace). However, in the recursive approach, once all c true normal vectors have been found, any additional vector constrained to be orthogonal to the previously found normals must lie in S — so this claim is correct. My initial worry was whether this is actually true, but on reflection, since the orthogonal complement of c linearly independent normals in R^D is exactly S (which has dimension d = D-c), any additional vector orthogonal to all previous estimates must indeed lie in S. This is fine. However, the more subtle issue is the contrast with the later claim about the proposed approach: the paper says when c' > c, PSGM returns 'c' − c redundant vectors that will still lie in S⊥'. The comparison with recursive DPCP is valid but the reader might wonder why the recursive method's extra vectors fall into S while the proposed method's extras stay in S⊥ — the distinction (orthogonality constraint vs. none) is what causes this, and this is indeed the paper's main point. So this is not an error, just a point that could be clarified. Not flagging as a real issue.

comments.title: Claim about linear independence when c' ≤ c is unjustified and possibly incorrect as stated
comments.quote: These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ . If _c[′] > c_ then PSGM will return _c[′] − c_ redundant vectors that will still lie in _S⊥_ yet they will be linearly dependent
comments.explanation: Initially I read this as a natural description of the expected behavior. But on closer reading, the claim that the PSGM-returned normal vectors are 'all linearly independent when c' ≤ c' is asserted without justification in this overview. Later (Lemma 4) the paper shows the recovered vectors are of the form PS⊥(b_i^0)/||PS⊥(b_i^0)||, which for random initializations in general position are indeed linearly independent almost surely when c' ≤ c. Also, the statement about c' > c yielding 'c' − c redundant vectors' is a slightly loose way of saying rank(B̂*) = c; strictly speaking, any c' − c of the c' vectors could be viewed as redundant since all lie in a c-dimensional space. This is informal overview language and is clarified later, so it is a minor framing issue rather than an error, but readers could be misled into thinking a specific c' − c subset is redundant.

comments.title: Inconsistent sphere dimension in c_{X,min} definition
comments.explanation: The definition of c_{X,min} uses minimization over b ∈ S^{d-1} ∩ S, while c_{X,max} uses b ∈ S^{D-1} ∩ S. Since S is a d-dimensional subspace of R^D, the intersection S^{D-1} ∩ S is the unit sphere within S, which is naturally (d-1)-dimensional. So both should be equivalent, but the notation is inconsistent between min and max. Looking at the original Zhu et al. (2018) paper, the permeance statistic is defined with b ∈ S^{D-1} ∩ S for both. The 'd-1' in the min version appears to be an OCR error or typo — either way, the inconsistent exponents (d-1 vs D-1) across the two closely related definitions could confuse a reader trying to match these quantities to the referenced literature.

comments.title: Incomplete/unclear projection equation (11)
comments.explanation: Equation (11) claims P_{S^⊥}(b^{k+1}_i) = (1 - μ^k_i p c_D) P_{S^⊥}(b̂^k_i). For this to hold, the subgradient contribution from the outlier term, when projected onto S^⊥, must equal μ^k p c_D P_{S^⊥}(b̂^k_i). This relies on the continuous-case identity E[o sgn(o^T b)] = c_D b for uniform outliers, but only the component in S^⊥ is retained. The derivation also implicitly uses that the inlier subgradient vanishes when projected to S^⊥ (since X^T b is the inlier contribution and inliers lie in S). These steps are nontrivial and the equation is presented without derivation. More importantly, for the scaling factor (1 - μ^k p c_D) to be positive (necessary for the direction-preservation argument that follows), one needs μ^k < 1/(p c_D), which is not stated as a condition. If μ^k p c_D > 1, the sign of the projection flips, and the 'scaled version' interpretation fails. This boundary condition is not addressed.

comments.title: Lemma 4 conclusion glosses over normalization issue
comments.explanation: Lemma 4 asserts that PSGM iterates converge to $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}^*_i = P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}^0_i)/\|P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}^0_i)\|_2$. This requires $P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}^0_i)\neq 0$, i.e., the initialization does not lie entirely within $\mathcal{S}$. For a spherical distribution this happens almost surely (if $c<D$, the subspace $\mathcal{S}$ is measure-zero), which is presumably why the 'almost surely' qualifier appears. This is acceptable, but the statement would be cleaner if it explicitly excluded the measure-zero event $P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}^0_i)=0$. The 'almost surely' language covers this implicitly, so this is a minor framing issue only.

comments.title: Step (11) only treats outlier term, not inlier term
comments.explanation: Equation (11) as written seems to use only the outlier contribution ($pc_D$). In the continuous setting, the PSGM subgradient step (10) has both an outlier term (weighted by $p$) and an inlier term. When projected onto $\mathcal{S}^\perp$, the inlier term $c_d \hat{\boldsymbol{s}}_i$ vanishes because $\hat{\boldsymbol{s}}_i = P_\mathcal{S}(\boldsymbol{b}_i) \in \mathcal{S}$, so $P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\hat{\boldsymbol{s}}_i)=0$. Hence only the outlier part remains after projection, and the formula is correct. This is a subtle but important step that a reader might not immediately see; a one-line justification would help, but the claim itself is correct.

comments.title: Theorem 7 / Lemma 6 sufficient condition based on $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{\Delta}}\|_2$ vs smallest singular value of $\boldsymbol{B}^0$
comments.explanation: This is a standard Weyl-type bound: if $\sigma_{c'}(\boldsymbol{B}^0)>\|\hat{\boldsymbol{\Delta}}\|_2$ then $\boldsymbol{A}=\boldsymbol{B}^0-\hat{\boldsymbol{\Delta}}$ has $\sigma_{c'}(\boldsymbol{A})>0$ and is full column rank. However, the theorem then claims rank$(\boldsymbol{B}^*)=c$ (not $c'$), where $\boldsymbol{B}^* = P_{\mathbb{S}^{D-1}}(P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\boldsymbol{B}^0-\hat{\boldsymbol{\Delta}}))$. Full rank of $\boldsymbol{A}\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times c'}$ (i.e., rank $c'$) only guarantees rank$(P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\boldsymbol{A}))\geq c' - d$ in general, but since the columns of $\boldsymbol{B}^*$ lie in $\mathcal{S}^\perp$ (which has dimension $c$), the image has rank at most $c$. The argument implicitly requires that $P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\boldsymbol{A})$ has rank exactly $c$, which follows from rank$(\boldsymbol{A})=c'\geq c$ only if $\boldsymbol{A}$'s column space has trivial intersection with $\mathcal{S}$... but actually, for a generic $\boldsymbol{A}$ of rank $c'\geq c$ in $\mathbb{R}^D$, $P_{\mathcal{S}^\perp}(\boldsymbol{A})$ has rank exactly $c$ almost surely. The passage skips this step. A reader could reasonably be confused about why full-rank of $\boldsymbol{A}$ implies $\boldsymbol{B}^*$ spans $\mathcal{S}^\perp$; the additional argument (genericity / measure-zero exception) is not spelled out.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and well-motivated limitation of prior DPCP-based robust subspace recovery methods: the need to know the subspace codimension in advance, especially in the high relative-dimension regime where many convex RSR approaches are weak. The central idea—running multiple independently initialized codimension-one DPCP/PSGM instances and relying on the algorithm’s implicit bias to recover a diverse spanning set of the orthogonal complement—is elegant, simple, and potentially impactful, and the introduction does a good job situating the contribution relative to recursive and orthogonality-constrained DPCP formulations. The writing is generally clear and technically mature, though the exposition would benefit from earlier, sharper intuition for why random initialization plus projected subgradient dynamics should induce “implicit rank regularization” rather than producing redundant normals or spurious in-subspace directions. The most significant issues to scrutinize are the strength and practicality of the theoretical conditions—e.g., distributional assumptions on inliers/outliers, required sample complexity, probability of obtaining a full spanning set as a function of the overestimated codimension, and sensitivity to initialization, step-size choices, and stopping criteria. It is also important that the paper clearly distinguish between knowing an upper bound on codimension and being fully dimension-free, and that it provide a principled criterion for estimating the recovered codimension from multiple solutions in finite-sample/noisy settings. Overall, the premise is strong and the contribution appears both conceptually interesting and practically relevant, but the paper’s persuasiveness depends heavily on whether the theory convincingly explains the claimed diversity of solutions and whether the empirical evaluation demonstrates robustness beyond idealized synthetic regimes.

comments.title: Ambiguity or reversal in the inlier/outlier ratio condition
comments.quote: Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) showed that the global minimizer of (1) is a normal vector of the underlying true hyperplane when both inliers and outliers are well-distributed _or_ the ratio between the number of inliers and number of outliers is sufficiently small.
comments.explanation: As written, this can be read as saying that recovery is guaranteed when the inlier-to-outlier ratio is sufficiently small, since the phrase lists “inliers” before “outliers.” That is counterintuitive for DPCP and inconsistent with the usual recovery intuition: absent benign outlier distribution assumptions, the inlier permeance term must dominate the outlier contribution, which requires sufficiently many/effective inliers relative to outliers. The preceding clause partly mitigates this because it separately covers well-distributed outliers, but the second condition still appears to have the ratio direction reversed or the numerator/denominator left ambiguous. It likely should say that the outlier-to-inlier ratio is sufficiently small, or equivalently that the inlier-to-outlier ratio is sufficiently large.

comments.title: Rank/spanning claims rely on projected random initialization, not ambient full rank
comments.quote:
Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a _scaled_ and _perturbed_ version of the initial matrix _**B**_[0] .

...

In order to guarantee that span( _**B**[∗]_ ) _≡S⊥_ it thus suffices to ensure that rank ( _**B**[∗]_ ) = _c_ . Here we show that a sufficient condition for this to hold is that the matrix _**A**_ = _**B**_[0] _−_ **∆**[ˆ] is full-rank.

**Lemma 6** _If σc′_ ( _**B**_[0] ) _> ∥_ **∆**[ˆ] _∥_ 2 _then matrix_ _**A**_ = _**B**_[0] _−_ **∆**[ˆ] _is full-rank._
comments.explanation: Several statements appear to conflate properties of the ambient initialization with properties of its projection onto the normal space. The limiting normal vectors should be controlled by the components of the initialization in S⊥, not by the full ambient matrix B0. Thus describing the discrete iterates as converging to a scaled and perturbed version of B0 itself is misleading unless the relevant object is actually the projection P_{S⊥}B0. More seriously, full column rank of A = B0 − Δ in the ambient space does not by itself imply that the projected columns span S⊥. A full-rank ambient matrix can have projected columns that are zero or linearly dependent in S⊥; for example, a nonzero vector lying entirely in S is full rank as a D×1 matrix but has zero projection onto S⊥. The continuous random-initialization intuition is saved by genericity of P_{S⊥}B0, but Lemma 6 only controls ∥Δ∥ relative to σ_{c′}(B0), not relative to a smallest relevant singular value of P_{S⊥}B0 or of the projected limiting matrix. The proof of the spanning conclusion therefore appears to need an additional argument controlling the projected rank, or the condition should be stated directly in terms of the projected initialization.

comments.title: Independence of recovered normals is stated too deterministically
comments.quote: Moreover, we observe that with random initialization we can get vectors sufficiently spread on the sphere that lead PSGM (initialized with those vectors) to return normal vectors of _S_ . These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ . If _c[′] > c_ then PSGM will return _c[′] − c_ redundant vectors that will still lie in _S⊥_ yet they will be linearly dependent (see Figure 1).
comments.explanation: The intended intuition is reasonable: in the ideal continuous analysis, each limiting column is essentially the normalized projection of its random initialization onto S⊥, so c′ such projected vectors are linearly independent almost surely when c′ ≤ c, while for c′ > c they necessarily lie in a c-dimensional space and are dependent. However, the prose states this deterministically: PSGM “will” return linearly independent normal vectors and exactly c′−c redundant vectors. This needs probabilistic and model qualifications—almost surely under the ideal continuous assumptions, and only under suitable finite-sample/perturbation conditions in the discrete setting. Otherwise it may sound like independence is an unconditional algorithmic guarantee rather than a consequence of random generic initialization plus later assumptions.

comments.title: The use of σ_{c′}(B0) and full column rank implicitly requires c′ ≤ D
comments.quote: **Theorem 7** _Let_ **B**[0] _∈_ R _[D][×][c][′] with columns randomly sampled from a unit ℓ_ 2 _norm spherical distribution where c[′] ≥ c with c denoting the true codimension of the inliers subspace S that satisfies Assumption 1. If_
comments.explanation: Theorem 7 and Lemma 6 use σ_{c′}(B0) and full column rank of a D×c′ matrix. This is meaningful as full column rank only when c′≤D. Since c is a codimension, D is a trivial upper bound, and in practice one would normally choose c′≤D. However, the formal assumptions only state c′≥c while describing c′ as an overparameterized upper bound. If c′>D, the c′-th singular value is undefined under the usual convention, or effectively zero if singular values are padded, and full column rank is impossible. The theorem should explicitly assume c≤c′≤D or reformulate the singular-value/rank condition for the intended range.

comments.title: Random-initialization recovery claim is conditional on an initialization-angle/basin assumption
comments.quote:
**Assumption 1.** _We assume that the principal angles θ_ 0 _[i][for][all]_ _**[b]**_[0] _i[s][satisfy][the][inequality][θ]_ 0 _[i][<]_ arctan  _NηNc_ _**XX**_ + _,Mη_ min _**O**_  _∀i, i_ = 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . , c[′] ._

...

Theorem 7 shows that we can randomly initialize DPCPPSGM, with a matrix _**B**_[ˆ][0] whose number of columns _c[′]_ is an overestimate of the true codimension _c_ of the inliers’ subspace and with columns sampled _independently_ by a uniform distribution over the unit sphere and recover a matrix that will span the orthogonal complement of _S_ . The probability of success depends on the geometry of the problem since condition (18) is trivially satisfied ...
comments.explanation: The theorem imports a convergence/basin condition through Assumption 1, but the later prose makes the random-initialization guarantee sound more unconditional, as though the only probabilistic bottleneck were the singular-value or conditioning condition in (18). Assumption 1 is an additional event on all sampled initial columns; it is not automatically guaranteed by uniform sampling from the sphere, and its probability can depend strongly on D, c, c′, and the angle threshold. As c′ grows, requiring every column to satisfy the basin condition becomes stronger. The theorem statement also phrases this awkwardly as “the inliers subspace S that satisfies Assumption 1,” although the assumption is about the sampled initial vectors. The success probability should either include this initialization-angle event or the result should be explicitly described as conditional on it.

comments.title: Spectral initialization does not specify the eigenvalue ordering needed for normal-vector initialization
comments.quote: Further, they are both initialized using of spectral initialization i.e., _**B**_[ˆ][0] _∈_ R _[D][×][c][′]_ which contains the first _c[′]_ eigenvectors of matrix _**X**_ **[˜]** _**X**_ **[˜]** _⊤_ as its columns, as proposed in Tsakiris & Vidal (2018).
comments.explanation: For DPCP normal-vector recovery, the usual spectral relaxation initializes with eigenvectors associated with the smallest eigenvalues of \(\widetilde X\widetilde X^\top\), since those approximate directions orthogonal to the data/inlier subspace. The phrase “the first \(c′\) eigenvectors” is ambiguous and, under the common convention that “first” or “leading” eigenvectors correspond to the largest eigenvalues, would describe the wrong initialization. The citation may clarify the intended ordering, but the text itself should specify smallest-eigenvalue, bottom, or trailing eigenvectors, or explicitly state the eigenvalue ordering convention.

comments.title: Figure 2 panel descriptions are inconsistent between caption and prose
comments.quote: Figure 2: Distances of the recovered _**B**_[[ˆ]] from the true orthogonal complements _S⊥_ as recovered by the proposed DPCP-PSGM algorithm provided an overestimated of the true _c_ i.e., _c[′]_ = 10 (left), RSGM provided the true _c_ (middle) and RSGM provided _c[′]_ = 10 (right).
comments.explanation: The caption gives a left-to-right ordering: DPCP-PSGM with \(c′=10\) is left, RSGM with the true \(c\) is middle, and RSGM with \(c′=10\) is right. The subsequent prose reportedly reverses the first two panel references, saying DPCP-PSGM is Fig. 2(middle) and RSGM with correct codimension is Fig. 2(left). This changes which visual result supports which claim, so the panel references should be corrected.

comments.title: Numerical codimension estimation criterion is underspecified
comments.quote: In Fig. 3 we report the estimated codimensions obtained by DPCP-PSGM for 10 independent trials of the experiments.
comments.explanation: The experiment is motivated by the prediction that the overparameterized output \(\widehat B\in\mathbb R^{D\times c′}\) should be biased toward rank \(c\), so estimating codimension from the numerical rank of \(\widehat B\) is natural. However, the text does not specify how the “estimated codimension” is computed in finite precision. PSGM returns approximate vectors, and even if the limiting object is rank deficient, the computed matrix will generally have small but nonzero singular values. A rank threshold, spectral-gap rule, normalization convention, or other decision criterion is needed to reproduce the reported recovery rates.

comments.quote: Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) showed that the global minimizer of (1) is a normal vector of the underlying true hyperplane when both inliers and outliers are well-distributed _or_ the ratio between the number of inliers and number of outliers is sufficiently small.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying that recovery is guaranteed when the inlier-to-outlier ratio N/M is sufficiently small, since the phrase lists “inliers” before “outliers.” That would be counterintuitive for DPCP and inconsistent with the usual recovery conditions: when the outliers are not assumed to be benignly distributed, the inlier permeance term must dominate the outlier contribution, which requires sufficiently many/effective inliers relative to outliers, not fewer. comments.title: Linear independence of recovered normals is stated too deterministically
comments.quote: Moreover, we observe that with random initialization we can get vectors sufficiently spread on the sphere that lead PSGM (initialized with those vectors) to return normal vectors of _S_ . These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ . If _c[′] > c_ then PSGM will return _c[′] − c_ redundant vectors that will still lie in _S⊥_ yet they will be linearly dependent (see Figure 1).
comments.explanation: I understand the intended argument: in the continuous analysis later, each limiting column is essentially the normalized projection of its random initialization onto S⊥, so c′ such projected vectors are linearly independent almost surely when c′ ≤ c; when c′ > c, they necessarily live in a c-dimensional space and hence are dependent. That is a reasonable high-level intuition. However, as written this sentence sounds deterministic: PSGM “will” return linearly independent normal vectors, and exactly c′−c redundant ones. This is only true with appropriate qualifications—almost surely in the ideal continuous model, and only under additional finite-sample/perturbation conditions with high probability in the discrete setting. Since the surrounding paragraph is introductory, the overstatement is not fatal, but a careful reader could reasonably wonder whether independence is a guaranteed algorithmic property rather than a probabilistic consequence of random initialization plus the later assumptions.

comments.title: Discrete-limit description appears to omit projection onto S⊥
comments.quote: Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a _scaled_ and _perturbed_ version of the initial matrix _**B**_[0] .
comments.explanation: The intended point seems to be the same implicit-bias mechanism described just above: the limiting columns are controlled by the random initialization, specifically by their components in S⊥. In the continuous result later in the context, the limit is the normalized projection P_{S⊥}(b_i^0), not a scaled version of the full initial vector b_i^0. Taken literally, convergence to a scaled and perturbed version of B0 cannot generally be correct, because a random B0 has nonzero components in S, while the desired limiting matrix should lie in S⊥; unless the perturbation is allowed to cancel an O(1) S-component, it is misleading to describe the limit as a perturbation of B0 itself. This would be clearer and technically safer if phrased as convergence to a scaled and perturbed version of the projection of the initialization onto S⊥, or if the later discrete theorem indeed uses a different precise object, that object should be named here.

comments.title: Ambient full rank is not sufficient to guarantee spanning after projection onto S⊥
comments.quote:
In order to guarantee that span( _**B**[∗]_ ) _≡S⊥_ it thus suffices to ensure that rank ( _**B**[∗]_ ) = _c_ . Here we show that a sufficient condition for this to hold is that the matrix _**A**_ = _**B**_[0] _−_ **∆**[ˆ] is full-rank.

**Lemma 6** _If σc′_ ( _**B**_[0] ) _> ∥_ **∆**[ˆ] _∥_ 2 _then matrix_ _**A**_ = _**B**_[0] _−_ **∆**[ˆ] _is full-rank._
comments.explanation: The preceding goal is exactly the right one: since the limiting columns are normal vectors, showing that their rank is c would imply that they span S⊥. What concerns me is the next reduction. Full column rank of A = B0 − Δ in the ambient space does not, by itself, imply that its projection onto S⊥ has rank c. A full-rank matrix can have columns whose S⊥ components are zero or linearly dependent; for example, with c=1 and c′=1, any nonzero vector lying entirely in S is full rank as a D×1 matrix but has zero projection onto S⊥. More generally, with c′≥c, ambient full rank can be caused by components in S while the S⊥ components fail to span S⊥. The continuous-case argument is saved by randomness/genericity of P_{S⊥}B0, but Lemma 6 only controls ∥Δ∥ relative to σ_{c′}(B0), not relative to a smallest nonzero singular value of P_{S⊥}B0 or of the normalized projected matrix. Thus the stated sufficient condition appears too weak for the claimed conclusion unless an additional argument shows that A remains in sufficiently general position with respect to S, or the bound is actually on σ_c(P_{S⊥}B0). This gap affects the logic leading to Theorem 7.

comments.title: The use of σ_{c′}(B0) implicitly requires c′≤D
comments.quote: **Theorem 7** _Let_ **B**[0] _∈_ R _[D][×][c][′] with columns randomly sampled from a unit ℓ_ 2 _norm spherical distribution where c[′] ≥ c with c denoting the true codimension of the inliers subspace S that satisfies Assumption 1. If_
comments.explanation: The theorem and Lemma 6 use σ_{c′}(B0) and full-rankness of a D×c′ matrix. This is meaningful as full column rank only when c′≤D. I understand that, since c is a codimension, D is a trivial known upper bound and in practice one would normally choose c′≤D. Still, the formal assumptions only state c′≥c, and the paper repeatedly describes c′ as an overparameterized upper bound. If a reader took c′ to be any overestimate, then for c′>D the smallest c′-th singular value is undefined under the usual convention, or effectively zero if one pads singular values, and full column rank is impossible. The result should explicitly assume c≤c′≤D, or otherwise reformulate the rank/singular-value condition for the allowed range of c′.

comments.title: The discrete recovery statement is conditional on an initialization-angle assumption, but the prose reads more unconditional
comments.quote:
**Assumption 1.** _We assume that the principal angles θ_ 0 _[i][for][all]_ _**[b]**_[0] _i[s][satisfy][the][inequality][θ]_ 0 _[i][<]_ arctan  _NηNc_ _**XX**_ + _,Mη_ min _**O**_  _∀i, i_ = 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . , c[′] ._

...

Theorem 7 shows that we can randomly initialize DPCPPSGM, with a matrix _**B**_[ˆ][0] whose number of columns _c[′]_ is an overestimate of the true codimension _c_ of the inliers’ subspace and with columns sampled _independently_ by a uniform distribution over the unit sphere and recover a matrix that will span the orthogonal complement of _S_ . The probability of success depends on the geometry of the problem since condition (18) is trivially satisfied ...
comments.explanation: The authors correctly import the convergence condition from the prior PSGM analysis through Assumption 1. However, the later interpretation of Theorem 7 makes the random-initialization guarantee sound as though the only probabilistic bottleneck is the singular-value/conditioning condition in (18). Assumption 1 is an additional event on all random initial columns; it is not automatically guaranteed by sampling uniformly from the sphere, and its probability can depend strongly on D, c, c′, and the angle threshold. In particular, as c′ grows, requiring every column to fall in the required basin becomes a stronger condition. The theorem statement itself also phrases this awkwardly as “the inliers subspace S that satisfies Assumption 1,” although the assumption is really about the sampled initial vectors. This could mislead readers into thinking the stated success probability fully accounts for random initialization, when it appears to be conditional on the angle/basin assumption unless that probability is included elsewhere.

comments.title: Spectral initialization does not specify the eigenvalue ordering needed for normal-vector initialization
comments.quote: Further, they are both initialized using of spectral initialization i.e., _**B**_[ˆ][0] _∈_ R _[D][×][c][′]_ which contains the first _c[′]_ eigenvectors of matrix _**X**_ **[˜]** _**X**_ **[˜]** _⊤_ as its columns, as proposed in Tsakiris & Vidal (2018).
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear: the baseline DPCP-type methods use a spectral initialization rather than the random initialization advocated for DPCP-PSGM. However, for DPCP normal-vector recovery, the usual spectral relaxation initializes with eigenvectors associated with the smallest eigenvalues of \(\widetilde X\widetilde X^\top\), since these approximate directions orthogonal to the data/inlier subspace. The phrase “the first \(c'\) eigenvectors” is ambiguous and, under the common convention that “first” or “leading” eigenvectors correspond to the largest eigenvalues, would describe the wrong initialization. This is not just stylistic: using the top eigenvectors would initialize inside the dominant data subspace rather than its orthogonal complement. The citation may resolve the authors’ intent, but the passage itself should specify “bottom/smallest-eigenvalue eigenvectors” or the ordering convention.

comments.title: Figure 2 panel descriptions are inconsistent between caption and prose
comments.quote: Figure 2: Distances of the recovered _**B**_[[ˆ]] from the true orthogonal complements _S⊥_ as recovered by the proposed DPCP-PSGM algorithm provided an overestimated of the true _c_ i.e., _c[′]_ = 10 (left), RSGM provided the true _c_ (middle) and RSGM provided _c[′]_ = 10 (right).

But the subsequent prose reverses the first two panels: it says DPCP-PSGM is Fig. 2(middle) and RSGM with correct codimension is Fig. 2(left). This is not plausibly an OCR symbol issue; it changes which visual result supports which claim. comments.title: Numerical codimension estimation criterion is underspecified
comments.quote: In Fig. 3 we report the estimated codimensions obtained by DPCP-PSGM for 10 independent trials of the experiments.
comments.explanation: The experiment is well motivated: because the theory predicts that the overparameterized output \(\widehat B\in\mathbb R^{D\times c'}\) should be biased toward rank \(c\), estimating the codimension from the numerical rank of \(\widehat B\) is a natural test. What remains unclear is how the “estimated codimension” is computed in finite precision. PSGM will return approximate vectors, and even when the limiting object is rank deficient, the computed matrix will generally have small but nonzero singular values. Thus a rank threshold, spectral gap rule, normalization convention, or other decision criterion is needed to reproduce the reported 100% recovery statements. If this rule is given in an appendix, the main text should at least point to it here; otherwise the numerical claim is underdetermined.
